questions.question: Explain the working principle of a raster scan display and mention its typical refresh rate.
keyPoints: Electron beam sweeps across each row (horizontal) and then moves to the next row (vertical)
Pixel data are stored in a frame buffer and read sequentially to modulate beam intensity
Full frame is refreshed repeatedly, usually at 50–60 Hz (or higher) to avoid flicker
Includes horizontal and vertical blanking intervals to allow beam to reset

questions.question: List two advantages and two limitations of raster scan devices compared to random scan (vector) devices.
keyPoints: Advantages: can display filled polygons, support true colour and shading; easier to generate complex scenes using frame buffer
Advantages: lower cost for high‑resolution colour displays; widely used in consumer monitors
Limitations: limited line‑drawing resolution; requires more memory for pixel storage
Limitations: slower update for pure vector graphics; potential screen flicker if refresh rate low

questions.question: Compare raster scan and random scan display technologies in terms of display technique, pixel addressing, refreshing, colour depth, suitable applications, and modern implementations.
keyPoints: Raster: matrix of pixels, row‑by‑row scan, frame buffer, refresh per frame; random: beam draws vectors directly, no pixel matrix
Raster supports high colour depth (8‑bit per channel) and complex shading; random limited to line‑only, often monochrome
Raster used in TV, monitor, LCD, LED, OLED; random historically used in CRT vector displays, CAD, oscilloscopes
Modern raster devices integrate double‑buffering, progressive scan, high‑refresh panels; random devices replaced by software vector rendering on raster hardware

questions.question: A computer graphics system uses a 1920×1080 raster display with a 60 Hz refresh rate. Calculate the time available for drawing each pixel row (including horizontal blanking), and explain how the refresh rate affects perceived flicker and brightness.
keyPoints: Total frame time = 1 / 60 s ≈ 16.67 ms; row time ≈ 16.67 ms / 1080 ≈ 15.4 µs (including blanking)
Higher refresh rate reduces flicker because the retina receives more frequent updates, smoothing perceived brightness
At 60 Hz, most people perceive a steady image, but fast motion can show tearing; higher rates (120 Hz, 144 Hz) improve smoothness
Brightness is also affected by hold‑time; modern panels use PWM or progressive scan to balance flicker and power efficiency
